The memory is intentionally retained for the lifetime of the window, not leaked; cleanup occurs elsewhere via refcount management.

The allocated memory is stored in curwin->w_alist, a persistent window field with reference counting (al_refcount). Ownership is transferred to the window structure and freed elsewhere via the refcount mechanism, not leaked locally.

The window allocated by win_alloc_popup_win() is stored into the persistent global array aucmd_win[auc_idx].auc_win, which caches and reuses the window across calls. Ownership is transferred to global state, so it is not leaked at function return.

The allocated buffer namep is stored into entry->es_name (the exestack entry), transferring ownership to the execution stack. The function VIM_CLEAR(entry->es_name) at the start shows es_name is later freed via the exestack lifecycle, so this is intentional ownership transfer, not a leak.

vim_strsave result is assigned to 'retval', a return-value variable, indicating ownership is transferred to the caller (standard Vim getnextac pattern where the caller frees the returned command string). No leak within this function; the static analyzer fails to track the cross-function ownership transfer.

The allocation from vim_strsave is stored into AUGROUP_NAME(i), an entry of the global 'augroups' growable array. Ownership is transferred to a persistent global structure and freed elsewhere during group cleanup, so it is not leaked within this function.

At the top of each loop iteration cmd is reset with VIM_CLEAR(cmd) (line 3077), which frees the buffer and sets the pointer to NULL. Any later free of cmd thus operates on a NULL or freshly-allocated pointer, so the allocation at 3203 cannot be freed twice across iterations. No feasible double-free path is shown.
